questions.prompt: On the dashboard pair, what physical quantity is the area under the speedometer curve up to time $t$?
hints: Slice time into small intervals — what's the distance traveled in each one?
Sum thin rectangles of (speed × dt) = total distance.
questions.explanation: Each thin vertical strip has area $v(t)\,dt$ = distance traveled in that tiny interval. Summing all strips gives the total distance — that's $\int v\,dt = s(t)$.

questions.prompt: And what does the SLOPE of the distance curve at time $t$ equal?
hints: Slope = rate of change = how fast distance is increasing.
questions.explanation: Slope of distance vs. time is the derivative $ds/dt$, which equals the instantaneous speed $v(t)$. Both halves of the FTC live in this dashboard.

questions.prompt: During the 'stop & go' trip, the distance curve has flat sections. Why?
choices: Because the car was driving backwards.
Because the speedometer reads zero, so the rate of distance change is zero — no new distance accumulates.
Because the FTC fails for piecewise functions.
questions.explanation: When the speedometer reads 0, distance isn't accumulating, so the distance curve is locally flat (slope 0). FTC: $\frac{ds}{dt} = v(t) = 0 \Rightarrow s$ constant.

questions.promptTex: \text{A car moves at constant 20 m/s for 8 s. What is the area under } v(t) \text{ from 0 to 8?}
questions.explanation: Area of the rectangle is $8 \text{ s} \times 20 \text{ m/s} = 160 \text{ m}$ — exactly the distance traveled. Confirms 'area = distance'.

hints: Speed is the derivative of distance.
questions.explanation: $v(t) = \frac{ds}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}(5t^2) = 10t$. So the speedometer reads $10t$ — speed is growing linearly. (This is uniformly accelerated motion: acceleration $\frac{dv}{dt}=10$ m/s².)
steps: **Recognize the physics translation.** 'Speed' is how fast position is changing — the DERIVATIVE of position with respect to time. So $v(t) = \dfrac{ds}{dt}$.

**Interpret physically.** At $t = 0$, $v = 0$ (starts from rest). At $t = 1$ s, $v = 10$ m/s. At $t = 2$ s, $v = 20$ m/s. The speedometer needle climbs at a constant rate. Constant *rate of speed change* = constant ACCELERATION: $a = \dfrac{dv}{dt} = 10$ m/s². Uniformly accelerated motion (free-fall-like).
**Bigger picture.** Position $s(t)$ → derivative → velocity $v(t)$ → derivative → acceleration $a(t)$. Three layers, each more about 'how it's changing' than the one before.
